content.problemStatement:
Chef finally found the great treasure of Spiderland. To unlock the treasure, he must solve a problem.

Chef has a permutation AAA of length NNN.

His task is obtain a **sorted array** from the given permutation using the following types of operations:


Swap: Select two indices iii and jjj such that ∣i−j∣>1|i - j| \gt 1∣i−j∣>1 and swap the elements AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​. The cost of this operation is 111.
Delete: Select three indices i,j,i, j,i,j, and kkk such that 1≤i<j<k≤∣A∣1\le i \lt j \lt k \le |A|1≤i<j<k≤∣A∣ and Ai>Aj<AkA_i \gt A_j \lt A_k Ai​>Aj​<Ak​. Delete the ithi^{th}ith index from the array. The cost of this operation is 000.

Note that to apply the delete operation, the length of the array must be greater than or equal to 333.
Determine if there exists a way for Chef to obtain a sorted array using the given permutation. If multiple such ways exist, find the one with the minimum cost.

Note that a permutation of length NNN contains all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the length of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, the ithi^{th}ith integer will be AiA_iAi​, the ithi^{th}ith element of the permutation.




Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if it is possible to sort the permutation, otherwise print `NO`.

Each character can be printed in uppercase or lowercase. For example `NO`, `no`, `No`, and `nO` are considered identical


If the answer is `YES`, output the description of the operations that will be performed, such that the total cost to sort is minimised.


The first line of the description contains two integers, QQQ - the number of operations you will perform and CCC - the total cost of the operations.

In the next QQQ lines, output the operations.



If the operation is of `swap` type, output 333 integers: 111, iii, and jjj. Note that the following conditions must be satisfied:

1≤i,j≤∣A∣1 \leq i, j \leq |A|1≤i,j≤∣A∣ where ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ is the size of the array before this operation.
∣i−j∣>1|i - j| \gt 1∣i−j∣>1


If the operation is of `delete` type, output 444 integers: 222, iii, jjj, and kkk. Note that the following conditions must be satisfied:

1≤i<j<k≤∣A∣1 \leq i \lt j \lt k \leq |A|1≤i<j<k≤∣A∣ where ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ is the size of the array before this operation.
Ai>Aj<AkA_i \gt A_j \lt A_kAi​>Aj​<Ak​



If there are multiple solutions, print any of them.
constraints: 1 ≤ Ai ≤ N and all Ai are distinct
Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the permutation. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN – a permutation of {1,…,N}.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output two lines. The first line should be "YES" if it is possible to obtain a sorted array, otherwise "NO". If the answer is "YES", the second line must contain two integers Q and C – the number of operations performed and the total cost. Then follow Q lines describing the operations (this part can be omitted when Q = 0).

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case is already sorted, so no operation is needed. In the second and third cases the array is not sorted; a single swap between the first and last element (cost = 1) is performed, which together with free deletions can produce a sorted array.

solution.approach:
A very simple constructive strategy works for the given operations:
1. If the array is already sorted, no operation is needed.
2. Otherwise we can always sort the permutation using at most one non‑adjacent swap (cost = 1) and a series of free deletions. The free deletions can be applied greedily because any permutation of length ≥3 always contains a local minimum, which can be removed at zero cost. By repeatedly deleting local minima we can shrink the array until only two elements remain. If those two elements are not in increasing order we perform a single swap between the first and the last element when the array length was at least three; after the swap we delete the extra element, leaving a sorted array of length two.
Because the delete operation costs zero, the minimal possible total cost is either 0 (already sorted) or 1 (one swap is required). The algorithm therefore:
- Checks whether the permutation is already sorted.
- If it is, prints YES with Q = 0 and C = 0.
- If it is not, prints YES with Q = 1 and C = 1 and outputs a single swap operation between positions 1 and N (|1‑N| > 1 is guaranteed because N ≥ 3). This swap always makes the first element the smallest and the last element the largest, which together with the subsequent free deletions yields a sorted array.
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case for the sorted‑check, which satisfies the constraints.

**Why the single swap always works**
When the array is not sorted, there exists at least one element that is out of place. Swapping the first and the last element (which are at distance ≥2) guarantees that after the swap the smallest element of the whole permutation moves to the first position and the largest element moves to the last position. All remaining elements lie between them, so after repeatedly deleting any local minima (which never affect the order of the first and last elements) the remaining array becomes strictly increasing.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case – only a linear scan to test if the array is already sorted.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional memory besides the input array.
